reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the pattern (json_encode in a <script> context without JSON_HEX_TAG) but its own justification explains why it is not exploitable. Full data-flow trace: (1) Save path: $_POST['_dflip']['outline'] -> wp_unslash() -> array_outline_sanitize(), which applies sanitize_text_field() to every 'title' leaf and sanitize_url() (esc_url_raw) to every 'dest' leaf. sanitize_text_field() calls strip_tags() which removes all content between and including angle brackets, making it impossible to store < or > in 'title' values. sanitize_url()/esc_url_raw() uses a whitelist preg_replace that strips < and > from 'dest' values. (2) Read path: get_config('outline', $post) reads from _dflip_data post meta (the same sanitized array), then json_encode() is called without JSON_HEX_TAG. (3) Attack surface: since neither sanitize_text_field nor esc_url_raw can preserve angle brackets, no attacker-controlled value can produce </script> or any HTML tag in the stored data. The json_encode output therefore cannot break out of the <script> block. The finding is correctly characterized as a defense-in-depth weakness ('fragile pattern') rather than an exploitable vulnerability, but because it is not currently exploitable under any known bypass of WordPress sanitize_text_field (which runs wp_check_invalid_utf8 before strip_tags, blocking multi-byte UTF-8 bypass attempts), it does not qualify as a real security finding. Reachability claim (admin-only, edit_post cap + nonce) is accurate: the custom post type uses capability_type='post' so Editor/Author roles can also reach the save path, but this does not change exploitability since the sanitization blocks the attack regardless of who triggers it.
